originalTitle: The main takeaways from Elon Musk’s plans for $1.75tn SpaceX flotation
summary: Prospectus for tycoon’s sprawling empire reveals his plan to keep control – and ambition to colonise Mars
content: Elon Musk’s SpaceX has revealed plans for a highly anticipated $1.75tn (£1.3tn) flotation next month as he seeks investor backing for his quest to make life “multiplanetary”. SpaceX is a sprawling business, encompassing the eponymous rocket launch company, the Starlink satellite broadband service, Musk’s xAI artificial intelligence startup and the social media platform X, formerly known as Twitter. Details about these businesses and Musk’s ambitions for them were laid out in a flotation prospectus. Here are some of the main takeaways from it. SpaceX is loss-making The entire business lost $4.9bn in 2025 on revenues of $18.7bn. Revenue is growing, however, rising by a third on 2024. SpaceX’s losses have widened since the start of the year, losing $4.3bn in the first quarter, compared with a loss of $528m in the same period last year. The company is split into three segments: space, which incorporates the rocket launch business whose clients include Nasa; connectivity, which houses Starlink; and AI, the unit behind xAI and the X platform. Connectivity makes the most revenue, at $11.4bn, followed by space with $4.1bn and AI at $3.2bn. The Starlink unit was the only profitable segment in the first three months of this year. The AI unit is heavily loss making, losing $6.4bn last year, which reflected factors such as higher computing expenses (for instance to build and operate the AI models that power Musk’s Grok tool). Capital expenditure was $20.7bn, with the xAI unit again showing itself to be a big cost centre. It accounted for $12.7bn of the total, primarily due to the cost of building massive datacentres. SpaceX has built the appropriately named Colossus datacentre. Mars ambitions There are some extraterrestrial aspirations in the document that are not found in the average flotation prospectus. The section marked “future markets” includes the following: space tourism; energy production and manufacturing on the moon and Mars; and asteroid mining. The prospectus acknowledges these markets “do not exist today”. “While we believe these industries will develop over time, the manner in which they emerge, including the timing of commercialisation, the scale and pace of adoption, and the applicable competitive, technical, regulatory, geopolitical, and economic frameworks may differ materially from our current expectations,” the document says, deploying the classic understatement of stock market legalese. But it is classic Musk to aim for the impossible. A more immediate ambition is datacentres in space, or “orbital compute” as the document describes it, powered by solar energy. If manufacturing on Mars is a long-term aim, this one is relatively short-term. The prospectus says it expects to launch extraterrestrial datacentres as soon as 2028. Musk will have 85% control of the business The world’s richest person and chief executive of SpaceX will control just over 85% of the voting power in the business, making it extremely difficult to unseat him from the company. Musk’s control will be derived from majority ownership of a type of stock known as class B, which carries much more heft than the class A stock that everyone else will own. In shareholder votes, each holder of class B gets 10 votes per share. Musk will be very much in control. Musk is in line to get even richer Musk, who is already worth about $676bn – more than double his nearest rival, the Oracle founder, Larry Page – stands to make a vast sum from SpaceX although the exact amount is unclear and there is quite the caveat to his maximum earnings. According to the prospectus, he has been granted 1bn class B shares that vest – meaning, Musk gets full ownership of them – if SpaceX manages to achieve the “establishment of a permanent human colony on Mars with at least one million inhabitants”. As well as colonising Mars, in order for those shares to vest he must always meet a number of market cap targets, referring to the total value of a company’s shares. Those targets stretch as far as getting the market cap to $7.5tn. Which is more than the combined economic output of the UK and Italy. Musk was also granted another tranche of B shares in March. These 302m shares vest if he hits targets including the completion of space-based datacentres delivering 100 terawatts of compute a year. Musk’s base salary, unchanged since 2019, is $54,000 a year. He famously bid $54.20 a share for Twitter in 2022, leading to speculation that he was referring to the slang term for cannabis, “420”.
chineseTitle: 埃隆·马斯克 (Elon Musk) 1.75 万亿美元 SpaceX 上市计划的主要收获
chineseSummary: 大亨庞大帝国的招股说明书揭示了他保持控制的计划以及殖民火星的野心
chineseContent: 埃隆·马斯克 (Elon Musk) 的 SpaceX 透露了备受期待的下个月 1.75 兆美元（1.3 兆英镑）的上市计划，他正在寻求投资者支持他的“多行星生命”探索。 SpaceX 是一家规模庞大的企业，旗下包括同名火箭发射公司、Starlink 卫星宽带服务、马斯克的 xAI 人工智能初创公司以及社交媒体平台 X（以前称为 Twitter）。有关这些业务的详细信息以及马斯克对这些业务的雄心壮志都在发行招股说明书中列出。以下是其中的一些主要内容。 SpaceX 正在亏损 2025 年整个业务亏损 49 亿美元，营收为 187 亿美元。不过，收入正在增长，到 2024 年将增长三分之一。自今年年初以来，SpaceX 的亏损扩大，第一季度亏损 43 亿美元，而去年同期亏损 5.28 亿美元。该公司分为三个部门：太空部门，其中包括火箭发射业务，其客户包括美国宇航局；连接性，其中包含星链；以及 AI，xAI 和 X 平台背后的单元。连接带来的收入最多，为 114 亿美元，其次是空间，为 41 亿美元，人工智能为 32 亿美元。星链部门是今年前三个月唯一盈利的部门。 AI 部门严重亏损，去年亏损 64 亿美元，这反映出计算费用较高等因素（例如构建和运营为马斯克的 Grok 工具提供动力的 AI 模型）。资本支出为 207 亿美元，xAI 部门再次证明自己是一个巨大的成本中心。其中占 127 亿美元，主要是由于建设大型数据中心的成本。 SpaceX 建造了名为 Colossus 的数据中心。火星野心 文档中有一些外星人的野心在平均中没有找到
whyItMatters: 这条信息属于全球新闻信号，适合用于观察外部环境、政策变化、地缘风险和市场情绪。
